comments.text: Another well known construction for infinitely many pairs of consecutive squarefull integers: if $(n, n+1)$ works, so does $(4n(n+1), (2n+1)^2)$.
By OEIS A076445, there are no such $n$ for $n \leq 73840550964522899559001927226 (\approx7.38\cdot 10^{28})$. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

J. She in this paper has proved that: There exist no consecutive powerful numbers of the form\[x^3 − 1 = p^2a^3, x^3, x^3 + 1 = q^2b^3\], where $p, q$ are primes and $a, b, x$ are integers. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
This should have the "powerful" tag (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

The latest improvements on this problem is done by Tsz Ho Chan in "A NOTE ON THREE CONSECUTIVE POWERFUL NUMBERS, 2025" (link here: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.21485) And by "Nonexistence of Consecutive Powerful Triplets Around Cubes with Prime-Square Factors, 2025" (link here: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2507.16828) (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
